ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for sous vide duck confit; FOODON_03302051 (duck food product) is the closest parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Duck meat) is the closest parent; no specific code exists for sous vide confit preparation of duck.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226934003 represents duck as a food substance; no specific confit or sous vide preparation code exists.

serving.description: 1 plate (bone-in duck leg with mashed potatoes, mesclun salad, seasoned vegetables, and black pepper sauce)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving consists of approximately 380 g total, comprising a bone-in duck leg (~180 g edible meat after bone), a portion of mashed potatoes (~100 g), mesclun salad and seasoned vegetables (~60 g), and black pepper sauce (~40 g).

nutrient_source.title: Duck, domesticated, meat and skin, cooked, roasted (USDA SR Legacy #05140) combined with component estimation for mashed potatoes (USDA SR Legacy #11371) and black pepper sauce
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for sous vide duck confit with mashed potatoes exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy duck meat and skin cooked roasted (FDC ID 05140, ~337 kcal/100g, protein 19g, fat 28g) blended with mashed potato component (FDC ID 11371, ~83 kcal/100g) and black pepper sauce, weighted by approximate serving composition. The composite per-100g estimate reflects the full plated dish including all components. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — mashed potato and mixed meat-potato meal analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sous vide duck confit with mashed potatoes. GI estimated at 52 based on the dominant glycemic contributor being mashed potatoes (GI ~87 per University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2021) diluted by the high fat and protein content of duck confit, which substantially lowers the effective GI of the composite dish. This is consistent with published evidence that fat and protein co-ingestion reduces postprandial glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as (52 × 42.6 g carbohydrate per 380 g serving) / 100 = 22.2, rounded to 23.8 accounting for sauce contribution. Estimation basis documented per Veritas GI policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg/100g; ~1216 mg per full serving) from seasoning, black pepper sauce, and confit curing process
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a serving and request reduced sauce to keep sodium intake below 600 mg per meal, as the full serving provides approximately 50–60% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Mashed potato component contributes significant rapidly digestible starch; estimated composite GI of 52 and GL of approximately 22–24 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that the mashed potato component raises the glycemic load of this dish to moderate levels; substituting mashed potatoes with a lower-GI side such as steamed vegetables or cauliflower mash would meaningfully reduce postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from duck skin and confit preparation (~3.4 g/100g; ~12.9 g per serving) and cholesterol (~68 mg/100g; ~258 mg per serving)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish occasionally and consider removing the duck skin to reduce saturated fat intake by approximately 40%, as the skin contributes the majority of the saturated fat and cholesterol content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sous Vide Duck Confit is a modern Western bistro dish that combines the classical French technique of duck confit with precision sous vide cooking. A bone-in duck leg is first cured with salt, black pepper, garlic, and thyme, then vacuum-sealed and cooked at a precisely controlled low temperature (typically 155–165°F / 68–74°C) for 24–36 hours, resulting in exceptionally tender, moist, and flavourful meat that retains more juices than traditional oven-rendered confit. The dish is finished by searing the skin to achieve a golden, crispy exterior. As served at The Tree Cafe (Funan, Singapore), it is plated with creamy mashed potatoes, a mesclun salad, seasoned mixed vegetables, and a black pepper sauce. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy, high-protein meal (~198 kcal/100g, ~13.5 g protein/100g) with notable fat content from duck skin and confit fat (~11.8 g fat/100g, ~3.4 g saturated). The mashed potato component contributes the primary glycemic load (estimated composite GI 52, GL ~23 per serving). Sodium is moderately elevated (~320 mg/100g) due to curing and sauce. Clinically, this dish is suitable for most adults in moderate portions but warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension (sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (glycemic load from mashed potatoes), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat and cholesterol from duck skin). The dish is not halal-certified in its standard form as duck confit is traditionally prepared with pork-derived lard in classical French cuisine, though modern adaptations may use duck fat only.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for sous vide duck confit with mashed potatoes and black pepper sauce exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy duck meat and skin (roasted) as the primary anchor, blended with mashed potato and sauce components weighted by approximate serving proportions. GI is an analogous estimate based on the mashed potato GI from the University of Sydney GI Database adjusted for fat-protein attenuation. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Cuisine tag in input JSON was listed as 'Coffee' (likely a GrabFood platform category for café-style restaurants); the canonical cuisine has been corrected to 'Western' based on dish identity and cuisine_tags array which includes 'Western'. 2. The food_name star symbol (⭐) has been removed as it is promotional menu clutter, not part of the canonical food name. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy duck entry and is not estimable from available component data. 4. Trans fat is reported as 0.1 g/100g reflecting naturally occurring trans fats (vaccenic acid) present in poultry fat at trace levels per USDA data; this is not industrially added trans fat. 5. Halal status is false by default as classical duck confit may use pork-derived lard; the specific restaurant preparation may differ but cannot be confirmed from available data. 6. Serving size of 380 g is an estimate based on typical Singapore Western café plating; actual restaurant portion may vary. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and are approximate. 8. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this composite dish.
